XCF Global shares reacted to expanded strategic plans and analyst commentary, as the company advanced a three‑party business combination with DevvStream and Southern Energy Renewables to build a vertically integrated alternative‑energy and tax‑credit platform, and detailed a tolling deal with BGN to replace a terminated Phillips 66 offtake pact, reshaping its commercial outlook. An analyst lifted the stock’s price target citing tangible progress on restarting the New Rise Reno SAF facility in line with prior assumptions and viewing the Axens technology collaboration as strong third‑party validation of XCF’s SAF strategy and long‑term production economics, while investors also weighed a major finance‑leadership reshuffle and the potential value of future 45Z clean fuel production credits.

data.title: Zions Bancorporation Earnings Call Signals Durable Momentum
data.description:
Zions Bancorporation, National Association (N.A) ((ZION)) has held its Q1 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 30% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

Zions Bancorporation, N.A. struck an upbeat tone on its latest earnings call, emphasizing robust year‑over‑year gains in profitability, margin and fee income, alongside exceptionally clean credit metrics and healthy deposit growth at quarter‑end. Management acknowledged softer quarter‑to‑quarter trends and seasonal expenses but argued that structural earnings power and strategy execution outweigh the temporary headwinds.

Strong Year‑over‑Year Earnings Growth

Net earnings climbed to $232 million, or $1.56 per diluted share, marking a 37% jump versus the same quarter last year. The improvement was driven by higher revenue, a lower provision for credit losses and a more favorable effective tax rate, underscoring the bank’s improved earnings quality.

Net Interest Income and Margin Expansion

Taxable equivalent net interest income rose 6% year‑over‑year to $662 million, with net interest margin improving 17 basis points to 3.27%. Management highlighted better asset yields and balance‑sheet repositioning as key supports for margin, even as funding costs remain elevated across the industry.

Steady Loan Growth Led by Commercial Lending

Average loans increased 2.4% on an annualized basis during the quarter and 2.5% year‑over‑year, with commercial lending as the primary engine. Underlying origination activity was stronger than headline numbers, which were dampened by mortgage sales and accounting changes.

Deposit and Funding Progress

Period‑end customer deposits rose by $1.3 billion, or 1.8%, from year‑end, offering investors comfort on core funding stability. Total funding costs fell 8 basis points sequentially to 1.68% as management repriced deposits and reduced reliance on higher‑cost wholesale funding.

Resilient Credit Quality

Credit metrics remained a key bright spot, with net charge‑offs at just 3 basis points of average loans on an annualized basis. Nonperforming assets fell to 48 basis points, while the 1.16% allowance for credit losses covered nonaccrual loans by a robust 239%.

Capital Strength and Tangible Book Growth

The bank’s Common Equity Tier 1 ratio held steady at 11.5%, providing a solid capital cushion for growth and capital return. Tangible book value per share surged 19% year‑over‑year, reflecting strong earnings and balance‑sheet normalization after prior rate‑driven pressures.

Fee Income Momentum and Capital Markets Build‑Out

Adjusted customer‑related noninterest income reached $174 million, up about 10% year‑over‑year excluding CVA, with Capital Markets cited as an outsized contributor. Management is investing heavily in investment banking, sales and trading and real estate capital markets, supported by strong pipelines heading into the second quarter.

Strategic Acquisitions and New Products

Zions agreed to acquire Basis Investment Group’s Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac lending programs, enhancing its CRE capabilities and agency platform. New offerings such as the consumer “Gold” account, a pilot small‑business product and strong SBA 7(a) growth signal a push to deepen relationships and diversify revenue.

Liquidity Management and Securities Cash Flows

The investment securities portfolio generated $493 million of principal and prepayment cash flows, of which $299 million was reinvested. With an effective price sensitivity of roughly 3.7 years, the portfolio is being used as a key on‑balance‑sheet liquidity lever and a tool to balance interest‑rate risk.

Positive Operating Leverage Trajectory

Adjusted pre‑provision net revenue reached $301 million, up 13% versus a year earlier, demonstrating operating leverage already running ahead of full‑year goals. Management reiterated its 2026 target of 100–150 basis points of positive operating leverage and noted that intra‑quarter trends looked even stronger than the reported average.

Quarter‑over‑Quarter Earnings and EPS Slippage

Sequentially, earnings fell 11%, with diluted EPS easing to $1.56 from $1.76 in the prior quarter, as revenue dipped and seasonal factors kicked in. Two fewer days in the quarter, sharply lower securities gains and typical first‑quarter compensation items were the primary drivers of the short‑term pullback.

NII and Margin Pressure Versus Prior Quarter

Net interest income declined $21 million sequentially, a 3% drop, as NIM compressed 4 basis points to 3.27%. Earning‑asset yields fell faster than funding costs, and loan repricing reflected earlier rate cuts, illustrating the lagged effect of the rate environment on quarterly results.

PPNR Moderation on a Linked‑Quarter Basis

Adjusted pre‑provision net revenue of $301 million was down 9% from the prior quarter, reflecting lower day‑count‑adjusted NII and fewer securities gains. Management framed the pullback as seasonal and rate‑driven rather than structural, pointing to the strong year‑over‑year improvement as the more important trend.

Expense Growth and Compensation Seasonality

Adjusted noninterest expense rose to $558 million, higher both sequentially and year‑over‑year, largely due to seasonal compensation costs. Additional spending on marketing, technology, outsourced services and incentives also pushed expenses up as the bank invests for growth and competitive positioning.

Average Deposit Softness Amid Competition

While period‑end deposits were higher, average deposits fell $540 million from the prior quarter, with about half driven by lower brokered balances and the rest by seasonal outflows. Management noted that deposit competition and repricing remain industry‑wide challenges that will continue to shape funding dynamics.

CRE Pricing and C&I Watchlist Concerns

The bank acknowledged pricing pressure across parts of commercial real estate, even as headline CRE credit metrics improved. In Commercial and Industrial lending, criticized balances ticked up slightly, with restaurants and other consumer‑exposed businesses singled out as segments requiring close monitoring.

Factors Masking Underlying Loan Growth

Reported loan growth was muted by the sale of more than $500 million of held‑for‑sale residential mortgages, which removed balances from the books. An accounting netting change affecting roughly $100 million of loans further constrained the reported figures, obscuring stronger underlying origination trends.

Capital Deployment and the CET1 Trade‑Off

CET1 stayed flat quarter‑over‑quarter as earnings were partially offset by $77 million of share repurchases, dividend payouts and higher risk‑weighted assets. Management noted that pending acquisitions, including the agency lending deal, will temporarily consume capital until approvals and integration are completed.

Guidance and Outlook

Looking ahead, management expects net interest income to grow about 7–8% over the next year, assuming no rate changes, with margin expansion and continuing loan growth as key drivers. Customer‑related fee income and noninterest expense are both projected to rise moderately, but the bank still targets 100–150 basis points of positive operating leverage in 2026, supported by strong capital, deposit growth and potential regulatory capital relief.

Zions’ latest call paints a picture of a bank leaning into growth and fee diversification while carefully managing credit and capital, even as quarterly earnings ebb with seasonality and rate noise. For investors, the combination of solid year‑over‑year momentum, low credit losses and a clear path to positive operating leverage offers a constructive medium‑term story despite near‑term volatility.

data.title: ServisFirst Bancshares Extends Margin Gains, Eyes More Growth
data.description:
ServisFirst Bancshares ((SFBS)) has held its Q1 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 30% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

ServisFirst Bancshares’ latest earnings call struck a confident tone, underscoring robust profitability, widening margins, and disciplined cost control despite a few idiosyncratic credit and income headwinds. Management argued that strong capital, core funding, and sizeable loan repricing opportunities should keep returns high and support further earnings growth even in a competitive, uncertain rate environment.

Strong Earnings Power and EPS Surge

ServisFirst posted net income of $83.0 million and diluted EPS of $1.52, or $1.54 on a normalized basis, up sharply from $1.16 a year earlier. Return metrics were standout for a regional bank, with return on average assets at 1.89% and return on average common equity at 17.91%, highlighting the profitability of its focused business model.

Net Interest Margin Expansion Fuels Revenue

Net interest margin expanded to 3.53%, adding 15 basis points sequentially and 61 basis points year over year as asset yields outpaced funding costs. Net interest income climbed to $148.2 million from $123.6 million a year ago, reinforcing that the core earnings engine is accelerating even before the bulk of loan repricing flows through.

Loan and Deposit Growth with Repricing Upside

Loans grew at roughly a 7% annualized pace in the quarter, while deposits rose at an 8% annualized rate, keeping the balance sheet both growing and balanced. Management highlighted about $2.0 billion of near-term low fixed-rate loans set to reprice, including $1.2 billion over the next 12 months at a 5.19% yield versus new loan rates nearer 6.5%, offering meaningful earnings leverage.

Sub-30% Efficiency and Tight Expense Discipline

The bank delivered an efficiency ratio of 29.81%, staying below 30% for the second straight quarter and underscoring its cost advantage. Noninterest expense rose modestly to $47.4 million, only 2.8% higher than a year ago, allowing strong operating leverage as revenues from spread and fee businesses outpaced the cost base.

Capital, Liquidity and Core Funding Strength

Capital levels remain robust, with a preliminary CET1 ratio of 11.86%, total capital to risk-weighted assets at 13.13%, and tangible common equity to tangible assets at 10.46%. Liquidity is ample with $1.84 billion of cash, roughly 10% of assets, and management emphasized that the balance sheet is funded entirely by core, relationship deposits without reliance on FHLB or brokered funding.

Growing Fee Income in Relationship Businesses

Fee income showed underlying momentum, led by service charges of $3.3 million, flat sequentially but up 29% year over year following pricing changes. Mortgage banking revenue rose 14% quarter over quarter to $1.9 million, while net credit card income advanced 12% year over year to $2.2 million, and core BOLI income grew 32%, supporting a more diversified revenue mix.

Strategic Texas Expansion Adds Growth Optionality

ServisFirst is investing in a Texas expansion, having leased 26,000 square feet for build-out, hired 18 bankers, and closed its first Texas loan in March. Over the past year the bank added 32 full-time employees, three-quarters in frontline roles, bringing total producers to 161 as management positions the franchise for long-term revenue growth in attractive markets.

Charge-Offs and Credit Cleanup in Focus

Net charge-offs were about $8.3 million in the quarter, largely tied to the final resolution of a single long-standing troubled borrower, limiting broader portfolio implications. Management said it remains heavily focused on workouts for remaining complex credits and views the recent loss as a step toward further derisking the book.

Nonperforming Assets and Ongoing Remediation

Nonperforming assets totaled 1.00% of total assets at quarter end, a slight uptick from 0.97% at the prior year-end, indicating a modest increase in problem credits. The bank expects roughly $17 million of NPA reduction in the near term from identified resolutions, suggesting continued progress but also some remaining asset-level cleanup work.

Noninterest Income Noise from One-Off BOLI Items

Noninterest income fell to $10.8 million from $15.7 million in the prior quarter, driven mostly by the absence of a $4.3 million BOLI death benefit recorded in Q4 and a $1.0 million prior-period BOLI adjustment in Q1. Management stressed that these items create volatility around an otherwise improving fee run-rate and should not be viewed as signaling fundamental weakness.

Expense Pressure from Texas Build-Out and Payroll

Salaries rose 13% sequentially and 17% year over year, reflecting new hires for the Texas initiative and seasonal payroll tax effects that inflated first-quarter costs. Executives guided to mid- to high-single-digit expense growth for the full year and indicated the efficiency ratio is likely to hover in the high-29% range as the bank continues to invest for growth.

Loan Yields and Competitive Pricing Dynamics

Overall loan yields slipped to 6.18%, down 11 basis points from the prior quarter, as the broader rate environment moved lower and competition intensified. Management noted aggressive pricing and credit terms in the market, which could pressure yields on new originations even as repricing of existing fixed-rate loans offers a counterbalance.

Explaining the Sequential EPS Step-Down

Diluted EPS eased to $1.52 from $1.58 in the fourth quarter, but the decline was wholly driven by one-time BOLI-related items rather than weakening core trends. Excluding the prior period’s death benefit and this quarter’s adjustment, underlying earnings continue to move higher on the back of margin expansion and solid balance-sheet growth.

Guidance and Outlook: Margin Tailwinds and Steady Growth

Looking ahead, management expects additional net interest margin expansion of roughly 7 to 9 basis points in a flat rate backdrop, fueled by asset repricing on about $2.9 billion of low-rate loans over three years and ongoing deposit cost management. With expenses growing only in the mid- to high-single digits, a BOLI run-rate near $3.8 million, strong capital and liquidity, and continued credit cleanup, the bank anticipates further improvement in EPS and returns as its Texas expansion ramps.

ServisFirst’s call painted the picture of a high-performing regional bank using its strong capital, efficiency, and core deposit base to press its advantage while absorbing targeted investment and cleanup costs. For investors, the key takeaway is that growing margin, disciplined growth in loans and deposits, and a measured Texas build-out are expected to sustain elevated profitability, even amid competitive pressures and modest credit noise.
